text: The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position. The surgical team prepares the skin and drapes the area, then creates an access port for the operation.

The surgeon deepens the colostomy into the abdominal cavity, creating a wide opening for the insertion of a glove port. Following the insertion, the colonic limb is mobilized within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection to lyse extensive adhesions between the abdominal wall and the small bowel and colonic mesentery.

The surgeon performs dissection of small bowel adhesions, a challenging step in laparoscopic Hartmann's reversal. An additional 5mm trocar is inserted into the right lower abdomen, later utilized as a drain port.
The surgeon uses endoshears to meticulously dissect a segment of the small bowel that is extensively adhered to the colonic mesentery, carefully separating the bowel from the mesentery.
Following the mobilization of the colon, the surgeon proceeds to the pelvic floor, identifying the rectal stump and using sharp dissection to clear nearby adhesions.
After completing sufficient dissection, the surgeon inserts an anvil through the glove port. A circular stapler is then inserted through the anus, penetrating the rectal mucosa for docking.
The surgeon fires the circular stapler, completing the anastomosis between the rectal stump and the mobilized colon.
The surgeon performs an air leakage test and intraoperative endoscopic evaluation to assess the integrity of the anastomosis, confirming no signs of leakage and completing the operation.
